weekly_overview.raw_line: What to watch during the week of Monday 4/27 - this week is extremely busy , with a slew of central bank decisions , a lot of critical economic data , and a ton of earnings . The key macro events include the BOJ decision (Mon night), the FOMC decision (Wed afternoon), China’s NBS PMIs for Apr (Wed night), the ECB and BOE decisions (Thurs morning), the US PCE/GDP for Q1 and PCE for Mar (Thurs morning), and the US manufacturing ISM for Apr (Fri morning). The key US earnings reports include: Mon morning ( VZ ); Mon night ( AVB, CCK, CDNS , and NUE ); Tues morning ( GLW, GM, HLT, KMB, KO, SHW, SPGI, SYY, UPS , and ZBH ); Tues night ( BKNG, BXP, HOOD, MDLZ, NCPI, OMC, SBUX, STX, TER, TMUS , and V ); Wed morning ( ABBV, ADP, APH, CTSH, EME, ETSY, GD, GEHC, HUM, PSX, SWK, WING , and YUM ); Wed night ( AMZN, CMG, EQIX, F, GOOGL, KLAC, MAT, META, MGM, MSFT , and QCOM ); Thurs morning ( APD, BAX, BMY, CAH, CARR, CAT, CI, COP, HUBB, LLY, MA, MO, MRK , and RCL ); Thurs night ( AAPL, AIG, AMGN, CLX, COHU, DXCM, GDDY, SNDK , and WDC ); Fri morning ( AON, ARES, CL, CVX, EL, LIN, LYB, and XOM ); and Sat morning ( BRK’b ). The key European reports include: Tues morning ( Airbus, Anglo American, Barclays, BP, Novartis , and WPP ); Wed morning ( Adidas, Astra Zeneca, Deutsche Bank, GSK, Mercedes, Norsk Hydro, Santander , and UBS ); Wed night ( Universal Music Group ); and Thurs morning ( Arcelor Mittal, BNP, Schneider Electric, Soc Gen, Unilever , and VW ).

The key US earnings reports include: Mon morning ( VZ ); Mon night ( AVB, CCK, CDNS , and NUE ); Tues morning ( GLW, GM, HLT, KMB, KO, SHW, SPGI, SYY, UPS , and ZBH ); Tues night ( BKNG, BXP, HOOD, MDLZ, NCPI, OMC, SBUX, STX, TER, TMUS , and V ); Wed morning ( ABBV, ADP, APH, CTSH, EME, ETSY, GD, GEHC, HUM, PSX, SWK, WING , and YUM ); Wed night ( AMZN, CMG, EQIX, F, GOOGL, KLAC, MAT, META, MGM, MSFT , and QCOM ); Thurs morning ( APD, BAX, BMY, CAH, CARR, CAT, CI, COP, HUBB, LLY, MA, MO, MRK , and RCL ); Thurs night ( AAPL, AIG, AMGN, CLX, COHU, DXCM, GDDY, SNDK , and WDC ); Fri morning ( AON, ARES, CL, CVX, EL, LIN, LYB, and XOM ); and Sat morning ( BRK’b ). The key European reports include: Tues morning ( Airbus, Anglo American, Barclays, BP, Novartis , and WPP ); Wed morning ( Adidas, Astra Zeneca, Deutsche Bank, GSK, Mercedes, Norsk Hydro, Santander , and UBS ); Wed night ( Universal Music Group ); and Thurs morning ( Arcelor Mittal, BNP, Schneider Electric, Soc Gen, Unilever , and VW ).

source_subject: Vital Knowledge: Vital Dawn for Thursday April 23, 2026
market_levels:
Market levels – the US equity futures are seeing pressure so far this morning: S&P -44 points/61bp, Dow -335 points/68bp, Nasdaq -184 points/68bp, and R2 K -22 points/80bp. The major Eurozone indices are down 40-75bp, with underperformance in autos, tech, banks, insurance, and media while utilities, energy, telecom, healthcare, and consumer staples outperform . The following stocks are outperforming on earnings: BE Semiconductor, Dassault Systems, L’Oreal, Nestle, Roche, Safran, Sanofi, Schindler , and STM . The following stocks are underperforming on earnings : Essilor Luxottica, Heineken, Man Group , and Sainsbury . Stocks in Asia generally saw losses on Thursday (Korea was an upside standout): TPX -0.76%, NKY -0.75%, Hang Seng -0.95%, HSCEI -0.79%, SHCOMP -0.32%, Shenzhen -1.05%, Taiwan TAIEX -0.43%, Korea KOSPI +0.9%, Australia ASX 200 -0.57%, and India is off 70-90bp. Treasury yields are up 1-2bp across the curve while the market prices in 6bp worth of Fed cuts for the year. The DXY is flat-to-up small. Brent is advancing again, rallying ~200bp to ~$104. Gold is sliding 80bp while silver drops 325bp. Bitcoin is dipping 120bp (to ~$77.5 K).
•
whats_happening:
What’s happening this morning – we didn’t see anything all that incremental on Iran overnight. Our view remains that a deal is likely, and it could come together fairly quickly (if both sides were to suspend their respective maritime blockades, a face-to-face meeting could be scheduled – this seems like the probable path forward ). Resolving Iran would be positive, but it’s also largely expected at this point, and will probably prompt a sell-the-news reaction (the anticipation right now is acting as an upside catalyst for equities ). For earnings , the big theme this morning is software vs. semis . The latter group saw a slew of solid-to-strong reports , w/ robust results/guidance from BE Semi, LRCX, SK Hynix, STM, and TXN ( STM and TXN are most impressive simply because expectations were lowest for both). Conversely, software will be under pressure today due in large part to NOW (which saw some deals slip out of the quarter due to what’s happening in the Middle East while margins will be diluted because of the Armis deal) and IBM (which posted a modest shortfall in software revenue). Note that both NOW and IBM (for software) increased their full-year guidance while Dassault Systems in Europe had a solid print, so the software news flow isn’t all bad . TSLA put up healthy results in Q1 thanks to strength in its auto business , but the problem is TSLA investors don’t think it’s an auto company (to justify the 200x PE multiple, the company needs to show a lot more progress on non-auto initiatives, and that didn’t occur in Q1. Meanwhile, the capex budget for this year was increased even further , which means 2026 free cash flow will be even worse than before). Industrial earnings Wed night were bullish ( CSX and especially URI ).
• What we’ll be watching on Thursday – the focus will be on earnings before the US open (we care most about AAL, AXP, BX, CMCSA, DOW, HON, KDP, LMT, PHM, TMO , and UNP ), the US flash PMIs for Apr (9:45am ET), the second round of peace talks between Israel and Lebanon , and earnings after the US close (we care most about BKR, INTC, NEM , and SAP ).
• What we’ll be watching on Friday – the focus will be on earnings before the US open (we care most about CHTR, HCA, NSC, PG , and SLB ) and the final Michigan sentiment report for Apr (the final numbers should look better than the preliminary ones as the initial survey was largely conducted before the Iran ceasefire).
• Market in a Minute (macro) - Eurozone flash PMIs for Apr came in mixed , with manufacturing outperformance at 52.2 (up from 51.6 in Mar and ahead of the Street’s 50.9 forecast) while services fell short at 47.4 (down from 50.2 in Mar and below the Street’s 49.8 forecast), while S&P notes that the overall economy struggled , with cooling growth (the flash PMI moved into contraction for the first time since late ’24) and surging inflation due to the fallout from Iran ( S&P ); it might take as long as 6 months once the war formally ends to fully clear Hormuz of mines ( Washington Post ); the head of the IEA reiterated his warning that the world faces the biggest energy shock in history because of what’s happening in the Middle East ( CNBC ); Wall Street regulators are increasing their scrutiny of the private credit industry to assess how much of a risk it poses to the broader financial system ( WSJ )
• Market in a Minute (micro – Thursday morning) - BE Semi shares outperform in Europe after posting robust Q1 results (including sales +28.3% Y/Y while bookings surged 104.5%) and providing bullish Q2 guidance (they see sales up 35% Q/Q vs. the Street forecast of +23.7%) ( BE Semi ); Dassault Systems shares outperform in Europe after the firm reported decent Q1 results and reiterated its full-year guidance (expectations were subdued and so the bar for an upside surprise was low) ( Dassault Systems ); Heineken shares underperform in Europe as investors express disappointment w/weak volume performance in the Americas (-2.6%) and Europe (-1.8%), although they did well in Asia Pacific (+10.1%) and the full-year guidance was affirmed (Heineken); L’Oreal shares spike in European trading after the company reported strong Q1 sales, w/adjusted like-for-like growth of 6.7% (far above the consensus forecast of +3.65%) and outperformance in all the product categories; LSEG shares trade decently in Europe after posting solid Q1 results and boosting its full-year guidance (it now sees revenue growth toward the upper-end of the prior forecast) ( LSEG ); Man Group shares underperform in London trading after the company reported neg. flow performance in Q1 (net outflows were -$1.6 B as the company witnessed a $6.1 B redemption from a single client in systematic long-only equity) ( Man Group ); Nestle shares outperform in Europe after posting solid upside on Q1 sales (+3.5% growth vs. the Street +2.6%) thanks to strength in coffee (+5.1%) and Food & Snacks (due to confectionery) ( Nestle ); Nokia shares jump in European trading after the company reported solid sales performance in Optical (+20%) thanks to strength in AI & Cloud customers (+49%) while the full-year guidance was left unchanged ( Nokia ); Roche shares trade well in Europe after the company reported healthy FXN sales growth in the pharma business (diagnostics fell slightly short) and reiterated the full-year guidance ( Roche ); Sanofi shares jump in European trading after the company reported solid Q1 results w/sales growth of 13.6% on an FXN basis (Dupixent sales surged 30.8%), and the full-year guidance was confirmed ( Sanofi ); STM (STMicro) shares jump in European trading after the company reported Q1 upside on sales and GMs and provided healthy guidance for Q2 (they see sales of $3.45 B vs. the Street $3.18 B, w/GMs of 35.2% vs. the Street 34.5%) ( STM )
• Market in a Minute (micro – Wednesday night) - CSX (CSX) reported solid EPS upside (43c vs. the Street 39c), w/the beat driven largely by impressive margin expansion (op. margins expanded 560bp Y/Y to 36% as operating expenses fell 6%), and the full-year guide is tweaked higher; IBM (Int’l Business Machines) reported upside on Q1 EPS (1.91 vs. the Street 1.81), w/the beat driven by higher sales (FXN revenue growth was 6% vs. the Street 5.2%) and margin expansion (GMs climbed 110bp and pre-tax margins expanded 140bp), and the full-year guide was left unchanged, but the closely-watched software business fell a bit short on the top line; INTC (Intel) – Tesla will become the first major customer for Intel’s 14 A manufacturing process ( Reuters ); LRCX (Lam Research) reported robust upside in FQ3 EPS (1.47 vs. the Street 1.37), w/the beat driven by higher sales ($5.84 B vs. the Street $5.75 B) and better margins, and the FQ4 guide is strong too; LUV (Southwest) reported Q1 EPS about inline w/the Street at 45c and the company seems to be executing on its turnaround initiatives well while benefiting from a healthy demand backdrop but as is the case w/all airlines, fuel is a massive headwind (the Q2 EPS guide mid-point is 50c vs. the Street 59c); LVS (Las Vegas Sands) reported solid upside on revenue (+25.3% to $3.59 B vs. the Street $3.34 B) and property EBITDA (+24.6% to $1.42 B vs. the Street $1.31 B), w/strength in both Macau and Singapore; MSFT (Microsoft) considered buying Cursor in recent weeks but didn’t make an offer ( CNBC ); NOW (Service Now) reported a modest shortfall in subscription revenue growth (+19% vs. the Street 20.4%; subscription revenues growth saw an approximately 75 basis point headwind from delayed closings of several large on-premise deals in the Middle East, due to the ongoing conflict in the region); SK Hynix reported solid upside on Q1 sales (+198% Y/Y to KRW52.5 T vs. the Street KRW50.8 T), GMs (79% vs. the Street 78.25%), and op. margins (72% vs. the Street 70%), and the qualitative commentary remains bullish (“As memory becomes increasingly critical in AI computing, demand for high-performance memory is surging while supply remains constrained, expect favorable pricing environment to continue for the time being”) ( SK Hynix ); TSLA (Tesla) reported solid upside on Q1 EPS at 41c (vs. the Street 38c), w/the beat driven by strong margin expansion (GMs rose 478bp while EBITDA margins climbed 183bp), and free cash flow was very healthy too, but sales fell slightly short due to pressure in energy generation/storage and mgmt. on the call raised its capex guidance for this year (which means free cash flow will be even weaker than previously envisioned); TXN (Texas Instruments) reported solid upside on Q1 EPS ($1.68 vs. the Street $1.37), w/the beat driven by higher sales ($4.83 B vs. the Street $4.52 B) and higher margins (GMs were nearly 200bp ahead of plan) as the company benefited from strength in industrial and data center, and the Q2 guide is bullish; URI (United Rentals) reported solid upside in Q1 on both sales ($3.985 B vs. the Street $3.88 B) and EBITDA ($1.759 B vs. the Street $1.68 B), and the full-year guidance is increased.
• Calendar for Thursday 4/23 – the focus will be on earnings before the US open ( AAL, AXP, BFH, BX, CBRE, CMCSA, CNP, DOV, DOW, FCX, HBAN, HON, IRDM, KDP, LMT, NDAQ, NEE, PCG, PENN, PHM, POOL, R, ROP, SNA, THRM, TMO, UNP, VC, VLY, WST ), US weekly claims (8:30am ET), US flash PMIs for Apr (9:45am ET), earnings after the EU close ( Accor, Vinci ), earnings after the US close ( ABCB, AMP, ASB, BKR, BYD, CHE, COLB, DLR, ERIE, GLPI, HIG, INTC, KN, KNSL, MXL, NEM, PFG, REXR, SAP, SLM, SSNC, VRSN ), Japan’s national CPI for Mar (Thurs night), and Japan’s services PPI for Mar (Thurs night).
• Calendar for Friday 4/24 – the focus will be on earnings before the EU open ( Eni, Holcim, K+N, Signify, Telia, Volvo AB, Yara ), the German IFO survey for Apr (4am ET), earnings before the US open ( CHTR, FHB, GNTX, HCA, NSC, PG, SLB ), and the final Michigan sentiment survey for Apr (10am ET).

Iran

• What bulls are talking about : it isn’t just that Trump (seemed to) extend the ceasefire in perpetuity , but by doing so he strongly demonstrated (once again) an extreme reluctance to escalate kinetically , removing a major risk from the table. Based on all the media reporting in the last 48 hours, it seems that if the US lifted its blockade for a brief period (even just a few days), Iran would sit down in Pakistan , and a deal could come together quickly (it appears that the two sides have narrowed their differences meaningfully on the key issues). Pakistani officials sounded hopeful (according to the NYT) about a US-Iranian negotiation happening within the next few days (Trump himself told the NY Post that a meeting might happen soon ). Trump thanked the Iranian leadership on social media (at ~1pm ET on Wed) for not proceeding with the planned execution of 8 female protesters. Karoline Leavitt said Trump doesn’t consider Iran’s seizure of two ships in Hormuz to be a violation of the ceasefire .
• What bears are talking about : Trump seems stuck in Iran , unwilling to escalate militarily , unable to exit completely , and forced to impose a Hormuz blockade that’s economically untenable for an extended period. The current Hormuz standoff is increasingly a tinderbox , creating the risk of unplanned escalation (the US and Iran are both interdicting ships, and the situation could easily spiral). The Israel/Lebanon ceasefire , which is critical to the US-Iran détente , feels particularly tenuous. The best-case scenario remains a vague MOU (not a final, absolute, and timeless deal) that reduces tensions temporarily but fails to address the long-term drivers of the present conflict. Even if Operation Epic Fury is past its kinetic peak (which isn’t guaranteed), the fallout from the conflict will be significantly stagflationary for several quarters (at least), and supply chains in the region will never fully return to pre-war status (anything flowing through Hormuz will be subject to a material risk premium going forward). According to a Pentagon assessment cited in a Washington Post article , it might take as long as 6 months once the war formally ends to fully clear Hormuz of mines. The head of the IEA reiterated his warning that the world faces the biggest energy shock in history because of what’s happening in the Middle East.

US macro

• With Jeanine Pirro continuing her investigation into Powell , the odds of Warsh being confirmed by the time Powell’s term technically ends are fading , which could set off a battle between the FOMC and White House (Trump might try to fire Powell if Warsh isn’t confirmed by the middle of next month).
• The Pentagon fired Secretary of the Navy John Phelan after months of feuding with senior officials , including Hegseth and Feinberg ( NYT )
• Republicans are growing concerned their redistricting initiative is backfiring ( WSJ )

International macro

• Australia’s flash PMIs for Apr saw M/M growth in both manufacturing (51, up from 49.8 in Mar) and services (50.3, up from 46.3 in Mar) (Bloomberg)
• Eurozone flash PMIs for Apr came in mixed , with manufacturing outperformance at 52.2 (up from 51.6 in Mar and ahead of the Street’s 50.9 forecast) while services fell short at 47.4 (down from 50.2 in Mar and below the Street’s 49.8 forecast), while S&P notes that the overall economy struggled , with cooling growth (the flash PMI moved into contraction for the first time since late ’24) and surging inflation due to the fallout from Iran ( S&P )
• Eurozone auto registrations for Mar jumped 12.5% Y/Y , a sharp acceleration from +1.4% in Feb (Bloomberg)
• India’s flash PMIs for Apr tick higher M/M , including manufacturing at 55.9 (up from 53.9 in Mar) and services at 57.9 (up from 57.5 in Mar) (Bloomberg)
• Japan’s flash PMIs for Apr were mixed , with growth in manufacturing (54.9, up from 51.9 in Mar) and a downtick in services (51.2, down from 53.4 in Mar) (Bloomberg)
• Philippines central bank hiked rates by 25bp in a preemptive strike against Iran-linked inflation ( WSJ )
• Singapore’s CPI for Mar was inline at +1.8% headline and +1.7% core (Bloomberg)
• South Korea’s Q1 GDP came in ahead of expectations at +1.7% Q/Q (vs. the Street +0.9%) ( Bloomberg )
• Taiwan’s industrial production came in a bit ahead of plan for Mar at +28.7% Y/Y vs. the Street +25.25% (Bloomberg)
• UK flash PMIs for Apr were solid , including manufacturing (53.6, up from 51 in Mar and ahead of the Street consensus of 50.3) and services (52, up from 50.6 in Mar and ahead of the Street consensus of 50) (Bloomberg)

Airlines/Travel

• AAL (American Air)/ALK (Alaksa Air) are in talks about revenue-sharing agreements and other strategic partnership but not a full merger ( Bloomberg )
• LUV (Southwest) reported Q1 EPS about inline w/the Street at 45c and the company seems to be executing on its turnaround initiatives well while benefiting from a healthy demand backdrop but as is the case w/all airlines, fuel is a massive headwind (the Q2 EPS guide mid-point is 50c vs. the Street 59c as fuel eats up very robust RASM performance at +17.5% ; fuel in Q2 is forecast at $4.10-4.15/gallon vs. $2.73 in Q1).
• LVS (Las Vegas Sands) reported solid upside on revenue (+25.3% to $3.59 B vs. the Street $3.34 B) and property EBITDA (+24.6% to $1.42 B vs. the Street $1.31 B), w/ strength in both Macau and Singapore.
• Spirit Airlines is nearing a deal to receive a $500 MM rescue from the US gov’t to prevent the airline from liquidating (Washington would take a sig. equity stake in the company) ( WSJ )

Consumer

• Essilor Luxottica reported a modest shortfall in Q1 sales growth at +10.8% (vs. the Street +11.2%) w/the miss driven by EMEA and Latin America.
• Heineken shares underperform in Europe as investors express disappointment w/weak volume performance in the Americas (-2.6%) and Europe (-1.8%), although they did well in Asia Pacific (+10.1%) and the full-year guidance was affirmed (Heineken)
• L’Oreal shares spike in European trading after the company reported strong Q1 sales , w/ adjusted like-for-like growth of 6.7% (far above the consensus forecast of +3.65%) and outperformance in all the product categories (Professional at +13.1% and Dermatological Beauty at +10.2% were particularly robust). “Despite current geopolitical and macroeconomic uncertainties, we are optimistic about the outlook for the global beauty market ”.
• LULU (Lululemon) named Heidi O’Neil as its next CEO, effective Sept 8 . O’Neil is a former Nike executive and left the sneaker giant last Sept after more than 20 years, most recently serving as president of consumer, product, and brand ( WSJ )
• Nestle shares outperform in Europe after posting solid upside on Q1 sales (+3.5% growth vs. the Street +2.6%) thanks to strength in coffee (+5.1%) and Food & Snacks (due to confectionery) ( Nestle )

Healthcare

• MEDP (Medpace) shares see pressure after earnings due to a soft B2 B and the retirement of the firm’s president (but revenue/EBITDA in the Q were modestly ahead of plan , and the full-year guidance was left unchanged ).
• MOH (Molina) reported solid EPS upside (2.35 vs. the Street 1.95), w/the beat driven by a favorable MCR (it was 100bp better than anticipated at 91.1%), but despite the better Q1 performance , they are keeping the full-year guidance unchanged .
• Roche shares trade well in Europe after the company reported healthy FXN sales growth in the pharma business (diagnostics fell slightly short) and reiterated the full-year guidance ( Roche )
• Sanofi shares jump in European trading after the company reported solid Q1 results w/ sales growth of 13.6% on an FXN basis (Dupixent sales surged 30.8%), and the full-year guidance was confirmed ( Sanofi )

Industrials

• CSX (CSX) reported solid EPS upside (43c vs. the Street 39c), w/the beat driven largely by impressive margin expansion (op. margins expanded 560bp Y/Y to 36% as operating expenses fell 6%), and the full-year guide is tweaked higher (they now see sales up ~5% vs. the prior 1-3%, w/ op. margin expansion near the upper-end of the prior +200-300bp range, and free cash flow up >60% vs. the prior “at least 50%”).
• HXL (Hexcel) reported solid upside on FQ1 EPS (59c vs. the Street 43c), w/the beat driven by higher sales and better margins, and the prior full-year guidance is reiterated . “The Hexcel team delivered strong first quarter results on rising commercial aerospace build rates supported by the normalization of channel inventory. We remain confident in our growth outlook and we are reaffirming our full year 2026 guidance despite geopolitical uncertainty in the Middle East and lingering commercial aerospace supply chain challenges”.
• PKG (Packaging Corp) reported solid upside on Q1 EPS (2.40 vs. the Street 2.15), w/the beat driven by favorable volume and mix in the legacy packaging segment, favorable labor and operating costs, and a lower tax rate , but the Q2 EPS guide falls short (2.33 vs. the Street $2.52) due to higher stock compensation expenses and benefits costs and a higher tax rate.
• URI (United Rentals) reported solid upside in Q1 on both sales ($3.985 B vs. the Street $3.88 B) and EBITDA ($1.759 B vs. the Street $1.68 B), and the full-year guidance is increased (they now see EBITDA of $7.625-7.875 B vs. the prior $7.575-7.825 B). “We reported first-quarter records in EPS, adjusted EBITDA and revenue, supported by healthy growth and solid execution across both our general rentals and specialty businesses”.

Financials

• FICO (Fair Issac) shares slump on Wed after Freddie and Fannie said they would now accept mortgages underwritten based on the Vantage Score 4.0 credit rating .
• LSEG shares trade decently in Europe after posting solid Q1 results and boosting its full-year guidance (it now sees revenue growth toward the upper-end of the prior forecast) ( LSEG )
• Man Group shares underperform in London trading after the company reported neg. flow performance in Q1 (net outflows were -$1.6 B as the company witnessed a $6.1 B redemption from a single client in systematic long-only equity) ( Man Group )
• UBS will be forced to increase its capital by $20 B under new rules from the Swiss gov’t (UBS issued a statement stating it “strongly disagrees” with the updated capital requirements ) ( FT , Reuters )
• Wall Street regulators are increasing their scrutiny of the private credit industry to assess how much of a risk it poses to the broader financial system ( WSJ )

M&A/Strategic Actions

• AAL (American Air)/ALK (Alaksa Air) are in talks about revenue-sharing agreements and other strategic partnership but not a full merger ( Bloomberg )
• Kakaku.com could be purchased by EQT AB in a deal worth nearly $3 B ( Bloomberg )
• MSFT (Microsoft) considered buying Cursor in recent weeks but didn’t make an offer ( CNBC )
• Softbank is seeking a $10 B loan backed by its stake in Open AI ( Bloomberg )
• Space X has held talks about a 3-way partnership between x AI, Cursor, and Mistral as Musk looks to bolster x AI’s position in the AI industry and compete more effectively against Open AI and Anthropic ( Business Insider )
• Spirit Airlines is nearing a deal to receive a $500 MM rescue from the US gov’t to prevent the airline from liquidating (Washington would take a sig. equity stake in the company) ( WSJ )
• Thoma Bravo will hand over control of software firm Medallia to its lenders, wiping out ~$5.1 B in equity ( Reuters )

TMT

• ASGN (ASGN Inc.) reported a large shortfall on EBITDA ($83.6 MM vs. the Street $96 MM) due to soft margins (sales didn’t deviate that much from expectations), and the Q2 guide is weak (they see EBITDA of $90 MM vs. the Street $110 MM).
• ASML (ASML Holding) shares saw pressure Wed afternoon after TSMC came out and said the chip equipment maker’s highest-end machines were still too expensive to deploy , which is why it won’t do so until 2029 ( Bloomberg )
• BE Semi shares outperform in Europe after posting robust Q1 results (including sales +28.3% Y/Y while bookings surged 104.5%) and providing bullish Q2 guidance (they see sales up 35% Q/Q vs. the Street forecast of +23.7%) ( BE Semi )
• CACI (CACI Int’l) reported upside on FQ3 EBITDA ($290 MM vs. The Street $275 MM) while sales were about inline , and the FY guide is tweaked higher (“We are raising our full year revenue guidance to reflect the addition of ARKA, and raising our EBITDA margin guidance to reflect the stronger performance of our organic business”).
• CCI (Crown Castle) reported a few cents of AFFO per share at 1.02 (vs. the Street 98c), and the prior full-year guidance was left unchanged . “We delivered a solid first quarter and remain on track to achieve our full-year 2026 guidance ”.
• Dassault Systems shares outperform in Europe after the firm reported decent Q1 results and reiterated its full-year guidance (expectations were subdued and so the bar for an upside surprise was low) ( Dassault Systems )
• IBM (Int’l Business Machines) reported upside on Q1 EPS (1.91 vs. the Street 1.81), w/the beat driven by higher sales (FXN revenue growth was 6% vs. the Street 5.2%) and margin expansion (GMs climbed 110bp and pre-tax margins expanded 140bp), and the full-year guide was left unchanged , but the closely-watched software business fell a bit short on the top line (sales up 8% vs. the Street 8.4%) due to weakness in transaction processing (up ~2% on an FXN basis). The full-year software sales guide is actually tweaked up slightly (it’s now “10% plus” vs. the prior 10%).
• INTC (Intel) – Tesla will become the first major customer for Intel’s 14 A manufacturing process ( Reuters )
• Kakaku.com could be purchased by EQT AB in a deal worth nearly $3 B ( Bloomberg )
• LRCX (Lam Research) reported robust upside in FQ3 EPS (1.47 vs. the Street 1.37), w/the beat driven by higher sales ($5.84 B vs. the Street $5.75 B) and better margins, and the FQ4 guide is strong too (they see EPS/sales of $1.65/$6.6 B vs. the Street $1.45/$6.04 B). The only problem w/LRCX is that expectations were extremely elevated, but fundamentally the company continues to perform very well .
• MSFT (Microsoft) considered buying Cursor in recent weeks but didn’t make an offer ( CNBC )
• Nokia shares jump in European trading after the company reported solid sales performance in Optical (+20%) thanks to strength in AI & Cloud customers (+49%) while the full-year guidance was left unchanged ( Nokia )
• NOW (Service Now) reported a modest shortfall in subscription revenue growth (+19% vs. the Street 20.4%; subscription revenues growth saw an approximately 75 basis point headwind from delayed closings of several large on-premise deals in the Middle East , due to the ongoing conflict in the region), the Q2 op. margin forecast falls short at 26.5% vs. the Street 30.3%, and the full-year op. margin guide is tweaked lower (now 31.5% vs. the prior 32%), but they outperformed expectations on c RPO growth and op. margins in Q1 , and the full-year sub revenue growth forecast moves higher (now 20.5-21% vs. the prior 19.5-20%). Part of the op. margin softness is a function of the Armis acquisition.
• PUBM (Pub Matic) issued a small upside preannouncement for Q1 , w/ sales o f $62.4 MM (vs. the Street $59 MM) and EBITDA of $2.5 MM (vs. the Street $174 K), and detailed some mgmt. departures . “Agentic AI is creating a structural shift that is redefining the entire digital advertising market. Our AI solutions are seeing the fastest adoption of any product launch in our history ”.
• STM (STMicro) shares jump in European trading after the company reported Q1 upside on sales and GMs and provided healthy guidance for Q2 (they see sales of $3.45 B vs. the Street $3.18 B, w/GMs of 35.2% vs. the Street 34.5%) ( STM )
• SK Hynix reported solid upside on Q1 sales (+198% Y/Y to KRW52.5 T vs. the Street KRW50.8 T), GMs (79% vs. the Street 78.25%), and op. margins (72% vs. the Street 70%), and the qualitative commentary remains bullish (“As memory becomes increasingly critical in AI computing, demand for high-performance memory is surging while supply remains constrained, expect favorable pricing environment to continue for the time being”) ( SK Hynix )
• Space X has held talks about a 3-way partnership between x AI, Cursor, and Mistral as Musk looks to bolster x AI’s position in the AI industry and compete more effectively against Open AI and Anthropic ( Business Insider )
• Thoma Bravo will hand over control of software firm Medallia to its lenders, wiping out ~$5.1 B in equity ( Reuters )
• TSLA (Tesla) reported solid upside on Q1 EPS at 41c (vs. the Street 38c), w/the beat driven by strong margin expansion (GMs rose 478bp while EBITDA margins climbed 183bp), and free cash flow was very healthy too (+$1.44 B vs. the Street -$1.8 B as they didn’t spend nearly as much on capex as anticipated, but Elon on the call reiterated that capex this year overall will be very elevated, coming in at >$25 B, which is more than the >$20 B guidance from Jan). Total revenue of $22.38 B fell a bit below the Street’s $22.73 B forecast due to pressure in the energy generation/storage business (where sales slumped 12% Y/Y to $2.4 B vs. the Street $3.6 B) while auto and services sales outperformed . Active FSD subscriptions spiked 51% Y/Y to 1.28 MM (up from 1.1 MM in Q4). “We saw continued growth in demand for our vehicles in markets in APAC and South America , while also seeing a rebound of demand in both EMEA and North America ”. “We are excited about Tesla’s positioning in 2026 with tailwinds persisting for the autos business , our continued progress on FSD , the ramp of Robotaxi (in Q1, paid Robotaxi miles nearly doubled sequentially; we continued laying the groundwork for expansion of our Robotaxi service to additional major U.S. metros), progress on Optimus ahead of mass production (Preparations for our first large-scale Optimus factory will begin shortly in Q2), and the growth of our energy production capacity ”. Bottom Line : this was def. a solid print, with particularly impressive margins and FSD subscription momentum, but energy storage (which had been a big part of the bull thesis lately) was notably weak, Robotaxi is still a drop in the bucket financially (and TSLA remains a big laggard here vs. Waymo and others), and Optimus is very much TBD. With a PE of ~200x, TSLA remains the world’s biggest meme stock.
• TXN (Texas Instruments) reported solid upside on Q1 EPS ($1.68 vs. the Street $1.37), w/the beat driven by higher sales ($4.83 B vs. the Street $4.52 B) and higher margins (GMs were nearly 200bp ahead of plan) as the company benefited from strength in industrial and data center , and the Q2 guide is bullish (they see revenue of $5.2 B at the mid-point vs. the Street $4.84 B).

market_in_a_minute_macro:
Market in a Minute (macro) - Eurozone flash PMIs for Apr came in mixed , with manufacturing outperformance at 52.2 (up from 51.6 in Mar and ahead of the Street’s 50.9 forecast) while services fell short at 47.4 (down from 50.2 in Mar and below the Street’s 49.8 forecast), while S&P notes that the overall economy struggled , with cooling growth (the flash PMI moved into contraction for the first time since late ’24) and surging inflation due to the fallout from Iran ( S&P ); it might take as long as 6 months once the war formally ends to fully clear Hormuz of mines ( Washington Post ); the head of the IEA reiterated his warning that the world faces the biggest energy shock in history because of what’s happening in the Middle East ( CNBC ); Wall Street regulators are increasing their scrutiny of the private credit industry to assess how much of a risk it poses to the broader financial system ( WSJ )
•
iran_section:
Iran overnight. Our view remains that a deal is likely, and it could come together fairly quickly (if both sides were to suspend their respective maritime blockades, a face-to-face meeting could be scheduled – this seems like the probable path forward ). Resolving Iran would be positive, but it’s also largely expected at this point, and will probably prompt a sell-the-news reaction (the anticipation right now is acting as an upside catalyst for equities ). For earnings , the big theme this morning is software vs. semis . The latter group saw a slew of solid-to-strong reports , w/ robust results/guidance from BE Semi, LRCX, SK Hynix, STM, and TXN ( STM and TXN are most impressive simply because expectations were lowest for both). Conversely, software will be under pressure today due in large part to NOW (which saw some deals slip out of the quarter due to what’s happening in the Middle East while margins will be diluted because of the Armis deal) and IBM (which posted a modest shortfall in software revenue). Note that both NOW and IBM (for software) increased their full-year guidance while Dassault Systems in Europe had a solid print, so the software news flow isn’t all bad . TSLA put up healthy results in Q1 thanks to strength in its auto business , but the problem is TSLA investors don’t think it’s an auto company (to justify the 200x PE multiple, the company needs to show a lot more progress on non-auto initiatives, and that didn’t occur in Q1. Meanwhile, the capex budget for this year was increased even further , which means 2026 free cash flow will be even worse than before). Industrial earnings Wed night were bullish ( CSX and especially URI ).
• What we’ll be watching on Thursday – the focus will be on earnings before the US open (we care most about AAL, AXP, BX, CMCSA, DOW, HON, KDP, LMT, PHM, TMO , and UNP ), the US flash PMIs for Apr (9:45am ET), the second round of peace talks between Israel and Lebanon , and earnings after the US close (we care most about BKR, INTC, NEM , and SAP ).
• What we’ll be watching on Friday – the focus will be on earnings before the US open (we care most about CHTR, HCA, NSC, PG , and SLB ) and the final Michigan sentiment report for Apr (the final numbers should look better than the preliminary ones as the initial survey was largely conducted before the Iran ceasefire).

Iran

• What bulls are talking about : it isn’t just that Trump (seemed to) extend the ceasefire in perpetuity , but by doing so he strongly demonstrated (once again) an extreme reluctance to escalate kinetically , removing a major risk from the table. Based on all the media reporting in the last 48 hours, it seems that if the US lifted its blockade for a brief period (even just a few days), Iran would sit down in Pakistan , and a deal could come together quickly (it appears that the two sides have narrowed their differences meaningfully on the key issues). Pakistani officials sounded hopeful (according to the NYT) about a US-Iranian negotiation happening within the next few days (Trump himself told the NY Post that a meeting might happen soon ). Trump thanked the Iranian leadership on social media (at ~1pm ET on Wed) for not proceeding with the planned execution of 8 female protesters. Karoline Leavitt said Trump doesn’t consider Iran’s seizure of two ships in Hormuz to be a violation of the ceasefire .
• What bears are talking about : Trump seems stuck in Iran , unwilling to escalate militarily , unable to exit completely , and forced to impose a Hormuz blockade that’s economically untenable for an extended period. The current Hormuz standoff is increasingly a tinderbox , creating the risk of unplanned escalation (the US and Iran are both interdicting ships, and the situation could easily spiral). The Israel/Lebanon ceasefire , which is critical to the US-Iran détente , feels particularly tenuous. The best-case scenario remains a vague MOU (not a final, absolute, and timeless deal) that reduces tensions temporarily but fails to address the long-term drivers of the present conflict. Even if Operation Epic Fury is past its kinetic peak (which isn’t guaranteed), the fallout from the conflict will be significantly stagflationary for several quarters (at least), and supply chains in the region will never fully return to pre-war status (anything flowing through Hormuz will be subject to a material risk premium going forward). According to a Pentagon assessment cited in a Washington Post article , it might take as long as 6 months once the war formally ends to fully clear Hormuz of mines. The head of the IEA reiterated his warning that the world faces the biggest energy shock in history because of what’s happening in the Middle East.
us_macro_section:
US macro

• With Jeanine Pirro continuing her investigation into Powell , the odds of Warsh being confirmed by the time Powell’s term technically ends are fading , which could set off a battle between the FOMC and White House (Trump might try to fire Powell if Warsh isn’t confirmed by the middle of next month).
• The Pentagon fired Secretary of the Navy John Phelan after months of feuding with senior officials , including Hegseth and Feinberg ( NYT )
• Republicans are growing concerned their redistricting initiative is backfiring ( WSJ )
international_macro_section:
International macro

• Australia’s flash PMIs for Apr saw M/M growth in both manufacturing (51, up from 49.8 in Mar) and services (50.3, up from 46.3 in Mar) (Bloomberg)
• Eurozone flash PMIs for Apr came in mixed , with manufacturing outperformance at 52.2 (up from 51.6 in Mar and ahead of the Street’s 50.9 forecast) while services fell short at 47.4 (down from 50.2 in Mar and below the Street’s 49.8 forecast), while S&P notes that the overall economy struggled , with cooling growth (the flash PMI moved into contraction for the first time since late ’24) and surging inflation due to the fallout from Iran ( S&P )
• Eurozone auto registrations for Mar jumped 12.5% Y/Y , a sharp acceleration from +1.4% in Feb (Bloomberg)
• India’s flash PMIs for Apr tick higher M/M , including manufacturing at 55.9 (up from 53.9 in Mar) and services at 57.9 (up from 57.5 in Mar) (Bloomberg)
• Japan’s flash PMIs for Apr were mixed , with growth in manufacturing (54.9, up from 51.9 in Mar) and a downtick in services (51.2, down from 53.4 in Mar) (Bloomberg)
• Philippines central bank hiked rates by 25bp in a preemptive strike against Iran-linked inflation ( WSJ )
• Singapore’s CPI for Mar was inline at +1.8% headline and +1.7% core (Bloomberg)
• South Korea’s Q1 GDP came in ahead of expectations at +1.7% Q/Q (vs. the Street +0.9%) ( Bloomberg )
• Taiwan’s industrial production came in a bit ahead of plan for Mar at +28.7% Y/Y vs. the Street +25.25% (Bloomberg)
• UK flash PMIs for Apr were solid , including manufacturing (53.6, up from 51 in Mar and ahead of the Street consensus of 50.3) and services (52, up from 50.6 in Mar and ahead of the Street consensus of 50) (Bloomberg)

Airlines/Travel

• AAL (American Air)/ALK (Alaksa Air) are in talks about revenue-sharing agreements and other strategic partnership but not a full merger ( Bloomberg )
• LUV (Southwest) reported Q1 EPS about inline w/the Street at 45c and the company seems to be executing on its turnaround initiatives well while benefiting from a healthy demand backdrop but as is the case w/all airlines, fuel is a massive headwind (the Q2 EPS guide mid-point is 50c vs. the Street 59c as fuel eats up very robust RASM performance at +17.5% ; fuel in Q2 is forecast at $4.10-4.15/gallon vs. $2.73 in Q1).
• LVS (Las Vegas Sands) reported solid upside on revenue (+25.3% to $3.59 B vs. the Street $3.34 B) and property EBITDA (+24.6% to $1.42 B vs. the Street $1.31 B), w/ strength in both Macau and Singapore.
• Spirit Airlines is nearing a deal to receive a $500 MM rescue from the US gov’t to prevent the airline from liquidating (Washington would take a sig. equity stake in the company) ( WSJ )
tmt_section:
TMT

• ASGN (ASGN Inc.) reported a large shortfall on EBITDA ($83.6 MM vs. the Street $96 MM) due to soft margins (sales didn’t deviate that much from expectations), and the Q2 guide is weak (they see EBITDA of $90 MM vs. the Street $110 MM).
• ASML (ASML Holding) shares saw pressure Wed afternoon after TSMC came out and said the chip equipment maker’s highest-end machines were still too expensive to deploy , which is why it won’t do so until 2029 ( Bloomberg )
• BE Semi shares outperform in Europe after posting robust Q1 results (including sales +28.3% Y/Y while bookings surged 104.5%) and providing bullish Q2 guidance (they see sales up 35% Q/Q vs. the Street forecast of +23.7%) ( BE Semi )
• CACI (CACI Int’l) reported upside on FQ3 EBITDA ($290 MM vs. The Street $275 MM) while sales were about inline , and the FY guide is tweaked higher (“We are raising our full year revenue guidance to reflect the addition of ARKA, and raising our EBITDA margin guidance to reflect the stronger performance of our organic business”).
• CCI (Crown Castle) reported a few cents of AFFO per share at 1.02 (vs. the Street 98c), and the prior full-year guidance was left unchanged . “We delivered a solid first quarter and remain on track to achieve our full-year 2026 guidance ”.
• Dassault Systems shares outperform in Europe after the firm reported decent Q1 results and reiterated its full-year guidance (expectations were subdued and so the bar for an upside surprise was low) ( Dassault Systems )
• IBM (Int’l Business Machines) reported upside on Q1 EPS (1.91 vs. the Street 1.81), w/the beat driven by higher sales (FXN revenue growth was 6% vs. the Street 5.2%) and margin expansion (GMs climbed 110bp and pre-tax margins expanded 140bp), and the full-year guide was left unchanged , but the closely-watched software business fell a bit short on the top line (sales up 8% vs. the Street 8.4%) due to weakness in transaction processing (up ~2% on an FXN basis). The full-year software sales guide is actually tweaked up slightly (it’s now “10% plus” vs. the prior 10%).
• INTC (Intel) – Tesla will become the first major customer for Intel’s 14 A manufacturing process ( Reuters )
• Kakaku.com could be purchased by EQT AB in a deal worth nearly $3 B ( Bloomberg )
• LRCX (Lam Research) reported robust upside in FQ3 EPS (1.47 vs. the Street 1.37), w/the beat driven by higher sales ($5.84 B vs. the Street $5.75 B) and better margins, and the FQ4 guide is strong too (they see EPS/sales of $1.65/$6.6 B vs. the Street $1.45/$6.04 B). The only problem w/LRCX is that expectations were extremely elevated, but fundamentally the company continues to perform very well .
• MSFT (Microsoft) considered buying Cursor in recent weeks but didn’t make an offer ( CNBC )
• Nokia shares jump in European trading after the company reported solid sales performance in Optical (+20%) thanks to strength in AI & Cloud customers (+49%) while the full-year guidance was left unchanged ( Nokia )
• NOW (Service Now) reported a modest shortfall in subscription revenue growth (+19% vs. the Street 20.4%; subscription revenues growth saw an approximately 75 basis point headwind from delayed closings of several large on-premise deals in the Middle East , due to the ongoing conflict in the region), the Q2 op. margin forecast falls short at 26.5% vs. the Street 30.3%, and the full-year op. margin guide is tweaked lower (now 31.5% vs. the prior 32%), but they outperformed expectations on c RPO growth and op. margins in Q1 , and the full-year sub revenue growth forecast moves higher (now 20.5-21% vs. the prior 19.5-20%). Part of the op. margin softness is a function of the Armis acquisition.
• PUBM (Pub Matic) issued a small upside preannouncement for Q1 , w/ sales o f $62.4 MM (vs. the Street $59 MM) and EBITDA of $2.5 MM (vs. the Street $174 K), and detailed some mgmt. departures . “Agentic AI is creating a structural shift that is redefining the entire digital advertising market. Our AI solutions are seeing the fastest adoption of any product launch in our history ”.
• STM (STMicro) shares jump in European trading after the company reported Q1 upside on sales and GMs and provided healthy guidance for Q2 (they see sales of $3.45 B vs. the Street $3.18 B, w/GMs of 35.2% vs. the Street 34.5%) ( STM )
• SK Hynix reported solid upside on Q1 sales (+198% Y/Y to KRW52.5 T vs. the Street KRW50.8 T), GMs (79% vs. the Street 78.25%), and op. margins (72% vs. the Street 70%), and the qualitative commentary remains bullish (“As memory becomes increasingly critical in AI computing, demand for high-performance memory is surging while supply remains constrained, expect favorable pricing environment to continue for the time being”) ( SK Hynix )
• Space X has held talks about a 3-way partnership between x AI, Cursor, and Mistral as Musk looks to bolster x AI’s position in the AI industry and compete more effectively against Open AI and Anthropic ( Business Insider )
• Thoma Bravo will hand over control of software firm Medallia to its lenders, wiping out ~$5.1 B in equity ( Reuters )
• TSLA (Tesla) reported solid upside on Q1 EPS at 41c (vs. the Street 38c), w/the beat driven by strong margin expansion (GMs rose 478bp while EBITDA margins climbed 183bp), and free cash flow was very healthy too (+$1.44 B vs. the Street -$1.8 B as they didn’t spend nearly as much on capex as anticipated, but Elon on the call reiterated that capex this year overall will be very elevated, coming in at >$25 B, which is more than the >$20 B guidance from Jan). Total revenue of $22.38 B fell a bit below the Street’s $22.73 B forecast due to pressure in the energy generation/storage business (where sales slumped 12% Y/Y to $2.4 B vs. the Street $3.6 B) while auto and services sales outperformed . Active FSD subscriptions spiked 51% Y/Y to 1.28 MM (up from 1.1 MM in Q4). “We saw continued growth in demand for our vehicles in markets in APAC and South America , while also seeing a rebound of demand in both EMEA and North America ”. “We are excited about Tesla’s positioning in 2026 with tailwinds persisting for the autos business , our continued progress on FSD , the ramp of Robotaxi (in Q1, paid Robotaxi miles nearly doubled sequentially; we continued laying the groundwork for expansion of our Robotaxi service to additional major U.S. metros), progress on Optimus ahead of mass production (Preparations for our first large-scale Optimus factory will begin shortly in Q2), and the growth of our energy production capacity ”. Bottom Line : this was def. a solid print, with particularly impressive margins and FSD subscription momentum, but energy storage (which had been a big part of the bull thesis lately) was notably weak, Robotaxi is still a drop in the bucket financially (and TSLA remains a big laggard here vs. Waymo and others), and Optimus is very much TBD. With a PE of ~200x, TSLA remains the world’s biggest meme stock.
• TXN (Texas Instruments) reported solid upside on Q1 EPS ($1.68 vs. the Street $1.37), w/the beat driven by higher sales ($4.83 B vs. the Street $4.52 B) and higher margins (GMs were nearly 200bp ahead of plan) as the company benefited from strength in industrial and data center , and the Q2 guide is bullish (they see revenue of $5.2 B at the mid-point vs. the Street $4.84 B).
